data.title: AMD's AI Roadmap Is More Promising Than Most Realize
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD)(TSX:AMD:CA) is a large US semiconductor player known for its expertise in high-performance computing, graphics, and visualization tech. While it has always been perceived by the market as being overshadowed by Nvidia, which holds about 80% of the market share, AMD's data center business has been experiencing explosive growth, as per its latest financial results.

The company's MI300x chip series, in fact, demonstrates superior accelerator and chip performance to leading products in the market, according to AI experts. Moreover, its ROCm suite seems poised to become among the leading software stacks in the AI space.

While, at first glance, AMD appears to be quite pricey, its growth prospects more than justify its multiples, which, in my opinion, puts the stock clearly as being in the buy zone. For these reasons, I rate AMD as a buy.

AI-Driven Wins Contribute to Strong 2024 Performance

2024 was a strong year for AMD on many accounts, and its recent results proved that its fundamentals are as strong as ever. The company clearly demonstrated that it has made significant strides in capturing share in both the PC and server markets, driven by a launch of leadership products in each category.

Revenue for the full year climbed 14% year-over-year, taking the total to nearly $26 billion, raising the sales benchmark even higher this year. This is a notable feat following the 4% decline reported in 2023. The jump back to a double-digit take-off, driven mostly by the data center business bodes quite well for the company, as bears earlier pushed the idea that AMD's growth was mostly tied to the Covid-era boost in demand for computers, as the work-from-home phenomenon exploded. The company's data center AI business has undoubtedly been a major growth driver, with annual revenue from this segment alone surpassing $5 billion in 2024. That represents nearly one fifth of the company's total turnover.

I believe that, like most big players in tech, AMD too is seeing its growth narrative increasingly being defined by its deliberate pivot towards catering to the AI-related markets. For instance, AMD's Instinct accelerator lineup continues to gain rapid traction with some of the biggest tech companies involved in AI. Meta, for example, has been exclusively deploying MI300X accelerators for the meta.ai Llama 405B frontier model, while utilizing Instinct GPUs to its Grand Teton platform. Similarly, Microsoft has also been using MI300X to power several of its GPT-4 models for Copilot, while also scaling up its GPUs into the magnitude of thousands, for the purposes of both training and inference. This adoption by top players in the AI game indicates that AMD's optimized silicon enjoys strong demand in one of the most competitive spaces in tech, and is thus very capable of powering next gen generative models and high-performance computing workloads.

The MI300x chip series is, in my opinion, proving to be a real game-changer for AMD's AI standing, and in many respects, giving Nvidia's perceived market dominance a run for its money. It clearly surpasses the performance metrics of the H200 SXM by several key measures:

Similarly, while AMD's feats on the hardware side are quite impressive on their own, I am also glad to see that the company has not been complacent on the software side of the AI race, with its ROCm software stack significantly enhancing the ecosystem of its offerings.

The company has been very vocal about its ambitions to have the AMD ROCm suite become the industry's number one software stack for AI. At present, over a million ROCm models are online on Hugging Face, and can be executed immediately on AMD’s hardware without the need for any additional configuration or modifications. Moreover, its systems are fully integrated with top machine learning frameworks such as PyTorch and JAX, further facilitating widespread adoption.

Overall, AMD's progress on the AI front has me quite optimistic about its growth prospects. The company itself forecasts the data center accelerator space to achieve a total addressable market of half a trillion dollars by as early as 2028, growing at a CAGR of 60%. I believe AMD stands pretty well positioned to capture substantial share of the market moving forward, with a comprehensive focus on both the hardware side and its highly competitive software suite.

Valuation Assessment

Now let's get to the valuation part of our assessment.

At the time of the writing of this article, AMD's market cap is about $181 billion. Based on this, let's have a look at how its valuation metrics fare:

As we can see, going by most metrics, AMD appears to be more inflated than the tech sector in general. For instance, its $26 billion revenue gives it a trailing PS ratio of about 7, which is more than double the tech sector median PS ratio of 3.3. Similarly, its trailing (GAAP) PE ratio of 112 is particularly concerning, given that the median ratio in the tech sector is less than 33. Its trailing price to cash flow ratio of 60 is also nearly triple the IT sector median ratio of 21.

The only metric which seems to suggest some undervaluation is AMD's non-GAAP, forward PEG ratio of 0.55, which is quite lower than the tech sector median ratio of 1.9. This implies that the stock is undervalued considering its growth prospects.

Broadly speaking, it would seem that AMD is more pricey than the typical pick in the tech sector. However, I would like to point out that AMD is a much faster grower than the average player in tech, as is visible in the chart below:

But do these growth rates justify the 'premium' we see in the stock's price multiples? That's difficult to ascertain at a glance. One way to find out would be to assess the general relationship the market assigns different picks in the semiconductor space. To do so, I have created a scatter graph to highlight this relationship, and assess where AMD stands. The results can be seen below, where I have highlighted some of the most notable plays of the industry:

As we can see in the chart above, the relationship between value and growth can be depicted in a linear fashion, as seen in the trend line highlighted. AMD's positioning above the line of best fit, in my opinion, is a good indication that the stock is clearly in the buy zone. If the market is consistent in valuing stocks based on their forward EPS growth rate, AMD's forward PE ratio should be close to 65, rather than its present level of 24. This would suggest a substantial value upside given its current performance and pricing.

Overall, AMD appears pricey at a first glance, but a look at its growth prospects, and its strong position in the AI data center space actually make it very attractive at its current pricing.

For me, this is a compelling indication that AMD is clearly undervalued, and therefore, I give the stock a buy rating.

Risk to Thesis

For the risk section of my analysis, I will elaborate on the one area that is on most people's minds. This relates, of course to the flat guidance on AI GPU sales in the first half of 2025. Given the monumental growth this side of AMD's business brought in 2024, this slowdown raises questions about the company's ability to sustain the explosive momentum it has just reported. This, of course, has triggered bears in the market to assume that AMD's extraordinary growth has arisen out of a one-off opportunity in the market, and thus should not be seen as a meaningful competitor to Nvidia.

While this pessimism may seem warranted, I do not assign it much weight for a number of reasons. Firstly, I don't believe that the slowdown reflects any underlying weakness in AMD's business itself, and rather points to a product-cycle timing issue. Over the long-term, I believe AMD's growth trajectory remains solid.

Moreover, the MI350 series is already showing strong early demand, due to which the company will initiate its production ramp earlier than previously anticipated. I believe that this accelerated timeline will give AMD a competitive edge, and a boost to its data center GPU momentum.

Overall, I am satisfied with AMD's efforts to better position itself to meet demand in a more sustained fashion, with the MI350 being a key driver.

Investor Takeaway

While, according to most market participants, Nvidia remains the dominant player in AI, I believe there is a strong case to be made that AMD is a lot more significant in this race than most give it credit for. While it may not necessarily surpass Nvidia in market share, in the short-term, its potential remains very attractive. Moreover, the DeepSeek phenomenon has equalized the playing field, in my opinion, which gives semiconductor companies like AMD a better fighting chance.

I rate AMD as a buy, given its strong AI opportunity, growth potential, and attractive pricing.

data.title: XP Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by XP Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: AstroNova: A Lot Of Work To Do To Right The Ship
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Atai Capital Management Q4 2024 Letter.

AstroNova (NASDAQ:ALOT) has often been our largest position since inception and one that I have discussed at length in prior letters. As a recap, the thesis hinged on a return to normalized earnings in both their test and measurement segment (tied to narrow body aircraft production) and their production identification segment (temporary ink/supplier/retrofit issues). Since our original purchase in January 2023, the business has performed as expected. At the end of their fiscal Q2-2024, the company (before Boeing strikes and shipment delays at PID, so a “semi-normal” quarter) was doing north of $21M in run-rate EBITDA – this compares to just $11M at the time of our original purchase. However, while the business has performed well, management’s poor capital allocation has cost us and taken away a good amount of our upside, for now at least.

In May 2024, AstroNova announced their $20M acquisition of MTEX, which we covered in our Q2- 2024 letter. At that time, it appeared that MTEX was a decent-to-great use of capital; it had high EBITDA margins (20%+), double-digit topline growth, some synergies, and perhaps most importantly, new technology that AstroNova’s legacy products could benefit from. Meanwhile, it also appeared they had paid around 10x 2024 EBITDA for the business, not bad.

Sadly, in their FY-Q2 report, many of these assumptions were proven false, with only the last point still valid today. MTEX in FY-Q2 put up a $1.4M operating loss and produced only $0.8M in revenue, and in the most recent quarter, FY-Q3, MTEX put up $1.7M in revenue and a $1.1M operating loss.

These numbers are obviously far from the assumed 20%+ EBITDA margins and well short of what we thought to be $12M+ in annualized revenue. To put it simply, management essentially took $20M of shareholder capital and threw it into a fire, and that fire continues to grow every day that MTEX remains unprofitable - not surprising in the least, the share price has declined materially off the back of this acquisition, which in turn made AstroNova our largest detractor for the year.

From our understanding, part of this poor performance is related to AstroNova doing a complete realignment of MTEX’s reporting structure, which covers everything from sales and marketing, manufacturing, technology, finance, and human resources – all of which now report directly to AstroNova leadership. Originally, MTEX was meant to be run far more independently after the acquisition. But once AstroNova got into the guts of the operations, they quickly realized that they needed to implement several changes and more closely integrate the business. One such example that led to this is that MTEX would frequently discount ink heavily, or in some cases offer it for free, just to sell more printers to customers (sacrificing higher margin long-tail recurring revenue for one-time lower margin revenue). This realignment has understandably slowed down the sales process for MTEX and further delayed the fulfillment of their backlog.

Furthermore, management on the recent earnings call discussed that they believe to have discovered specific details inconsistent with the information originally provided by the seller and are “seeking potential remedies from the seller under these agreements.” This is not at all surprising to us, given MTEX’s performance thus far. One needs not to look any further than MTEX’s supposed “annual reports” on their website from 2021/2022 and compare those financials to their performance thus far to understand that they are/were likely fraudulent, or at the very least presented in a misleading and false manner. Clearly, there may have been some nefarious and less-than-truthful things going on with what the seller was communicating to AstroNova. The only way you would acquire something like this is if you were to forgo necessary due diligence checks, and management has more or less alluded to doing so in the recent public call. The only logical conclusion I can come to for doing this is if there were other interested buyers (which management did not deny when asked on the original M&A call) also willing to forgo these crucial guardrails, making the deal time-sensitive and a potential bidding war. Regardless of how this came to fruition, it is apparent that neither the board nor management asked the right questions.

However, reading between the lines and given the timing of the old CFO’s “retirement” (roughly a month after the deal was announced), we can infer he was likely fired over this transaction. Still, even if we were to believe that the old CFO was responsible for this debacle, that does not relieve Greg nor the board of their responsibility in this matter as the buck stops with them as far as I’m concerned, and this acquisition should not have been made without proper due diligence.

So, where are we today, and what have we done about this? If it’s not clear from my writing thus far, I am frustrated with this outcome and would have considered selling some of our shares in AstroNova had it not been for my conversation with Greg and the new CFO, Tom DeByle, after FY- Q2. During this conversation and a few afterward, we pushed Greg relentlessly on this acquisition, what happened, why it happened, how we’re going to fix it, and, more importantly, how we will avoid another significant error like this going forward. Greg, luckily, took responsibility, and we believe he has learned from it. Self-reflection is very important as an investor and capital allocator, people make mistakes (ourselves included), so we are willing to forgive management this time around since there was an acknowledgement of this mistake and they have performed well excluding this incident. However, this transaction might more accurately be classified as a blunder today on part rather than just a small mistake. Sadly, it has negatively impacted us and all AstroNova shareholders, at least for now.

However, it was not Greg’s “self-reflection” that kept us in AstroNova; instead, it was the new CFO, who we believe is fighting well above his weight class. Tom was previously the CFO of Standex, a multi-billion-dollar public industrial company, from 2008 till late 2019. During his time at Standex, Tom oversaw earnings per share go from $1.49 to $4.72, EBITDA margin expansion from 8% to 15%, seventeen different acquisitions, numerous non-strategic divestitures, $40M in permanent cost savings, and a 400% increase in Standex’s share price.

From our research and conversations with him, Tom is an intense straight shooter and, in our opinion, acts as a good balancing act to Greg, who usually comes off as too stoic. During our conversations with Tom online and in person at Southwest Ideas, we heard him reiterate that sometimes “the best acquisition is the one you don’t make.” MTEX would have “ never happened in the way it did if he was here,” and “there will not be another acquisition like this one.” We like Tom and believe he is a massive improvement from the prior CFO (whom we never had a favorable opinion of). We believe that Tom likely has a lot of work to do to “right the ship,” and that will take some time, but we are confident that he will find many ways to cut costs, improve efficiency, increase inventory turns, and create value at both MTEX and AstroNova.

So, is there any silver lining to MTEX itself outside of us getting a much-needed new CFO? Yes, there is still a small possibility of MTEX being a meaningful contributor to AstroNova’s business looking a few years out. While we don’t know MTEX’s “true” earnings power today, management seems to believe they’ll have it back to profitability in 2025. More important, however, is the technology that came with this acquisition. We believe that AstroNova will be able to utilize MTEX’s printhead technology to help diversify and lower their reliance on ink suppliers, increasing margins and lowering supply-related uncertainty moving forward. Additionally, there are also some software programs they picked up; one such example is MTEX’s “TRAX” software, which increases visibility into customers' ink supply, maintenance, printer usage, and reduces friction for customers when restocking Ink. However, going forward and until proven otherwise, we are assuming no profitability for MTEX out of conservatism and instead assume the business gets to B/E this year or next – which seems feasible on its current trajectory. While there is still the slim possibility of this acquisition not being a total waste of capital, I am giving them no such credit and remain hopeful we’ll get some sort of compensation from the seller.

As of writing, the company has a market cap of $88M and an enterprise value of $137M. We believe they will do mid-to-high twenty millions of EBITDA in 2026 as narrow body production continues to ramp and their product identification business benefits from cost saving initiatives. Looking out to 2027, we see them doing around, if not upwards of $30M in EBITDA driven by $4M+ of Honeywell royalty expenses falling off and straight to the bottom line. These assumptions imply AstroNova is trading for less than 5.0x and 7.0x 2027 EBITDA and UFCF, respectively, before any incremental cash generation. These multiples seem overly draconian to us, and we will continue to strongly recommend that management implement a substantial buyback if shares remain depressed after deleveraging the balance sheet. However, if there should be further poor capital allocation, we will not hesitate to voice our opinion more openly towards both management and the board and believe there would be a substantial number of other investors who’d follow suit.

Brandon Daniel

Disclaimer:

This letter expresses the views of the author as of the date cited, and such views are subject to change at any time without notice. The information contained in this letter should not be construed as investment advice, and Atai Capital Management, LLC(“Atai Capital”) has no duty or obligation to update the information contained herein. This letter may also contain information derived from independent third-party sources. Atai Capital believes that the sources from which such information is derived are reliable; however, Atai Capital does not and cannot guarantee the accuracy of such information. References to stocks, securities, or investments in this letter should not be considered investment recommendations or financial advice of any sort.

Any return amounts that are reported within this letter are estimated by Atai Capital on an unaudited basis and are subject to revision. Atai Capital’s returns are calculated net of a 2.00% annual management fee and reflect a client’s performance who would have joined the firm on its inception date (01/03/2023). Actual Individual investor returns will vary based on the timing of their initial investment, the impacts of additions and withdrawals from their account, and their individually negotiated fee structure. Atai Capital believes showing returns net of a 2.00% management fee better reflects actual performance as of 02/17/2025 since no account that Atai Capital currently manages is charged a fee more than the stated 2.00% management fee. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Index returns referenced in this letter include the S&P500, Russell Microcap, and Russell 2000. Atai Capital’s returns are likely to differ from those of any referenced index. These returns are calculated from the respective provider’s websites, Essential Intelligence for the S&P500 and ftserussell.com for the Russell Microcap and Russell 2000, and include the reinvestment of all dividends in both cases.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Historical performance for the I Class prior to its inception is based on the performance of R5 Class shares. I Class performance has been adjusted to reflect differences in expenses between classes, if applicable.

Expense ratio is as of the fund's current prospectus. The I Class minimum investment amount is$5 million($3 million for endowments and foundations) per fund. The R5 Share Class is available only to participants in group employer-sponsored retirement plans where a financial intermediary provides recordkeeping services to plan participants.

Returns or yields for the fund would have been lower if a portion of the management fee had not been waived. Review the annual or semiannual report for the most current information.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Bonds retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the fourth quarter. However, a strong rally in the third quarter, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s(Fed’s) latest easing campaign, helped the market maintain a year-to-date gain.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Fed cut rates, altered its outlook. The Fed followed up its 0.5% September rate cut with two 0.25% cuts in the fourth quarter. This pushed the Fed’s rate target range to 4.25% to 4.5%. But amid persistent inflation, Fed officials adopted a more cautious stance, boosting their inflation and growth forecasts and reducing their rate cut outlook. They indicated future cuts would be data dependent.

Treasury yields rose. Despite a brief postelection rally, Treasury yields ended the quarter higher, and the yield curve between two and 10 years steepened. Above-target inflation, mounting U.S. debt and changing expectations regarding Fed policy contributed to the yield backdrop. The yield on the 10-year note climbed 0.79% to 4.58%, while the two-year Treasury yield increased 0.6% to 4.25%.

Inflation persisted. Inflation increased for the second straight month in November. Similarly, annual core personal consumption expenditures, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, rose to 2.8% in October and remained there in November. Inflation break-even rates increased, and Treasury inflation-protected securities(TIPS) outperformed nominal Treasuries.

All investment-grade bond sectors declined. Although credit spreads tightened in the quarter, rising yields pressured investment-grade corporate bond returns, which declined but fared slightly better than Treasuries and mortgage-backed securities(MBS). High-yield corporates advanced fractionally and outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Out-of-index positions contributed. Out-of-index allocations to the securitized and corporate sectors lifted relative performance. In the securitized sector, allocations to non-agency collateralized mortgage obligations, asset-backed securities, non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities and agency MBS were top contributors. Non-index investment-grade corporates also boosted results.

Inflation exposure aided results. We held an underweight position relative to the index in TIPS, which lifted performance as TIPS yields increased. We also held inflation swaps in conjunction with securitized and corporate bonds to manage the portfolio’s inflation exposure. This position boosted relative results as inflation break-even rates ended the quarter higher.

Duration positioning detracted. Entering the quarter, the portfolio had a slightly longer- than-index duration position, which weighed on results as yields climbed in October. By early November, we reduced duration to neutral, where it remained through quarter-end.

Portfolio Positioning

Economy may slow before gaining momentum. We expect slower growth in the first half of 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, likely weaken consumer demand and employment. By the second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may take hold, creating renewed economic momentum.

Inflation should ease. We believe inflation should gradually move toward the 2% target, enabling additional Fed easing—probably more than the market currently expects. Meanwhile, President-elect Donald Trump’s policy priorities, including tariffs, deportations and onshoring, could push inflation higher later in the year. But a strong dollar and energy sector deregulation may temper those effects.

Security selection remains key. Our research efforts continue to help uncover value and manage risk. Trade wars, inflation fears and rate volatility could lead to better valuations and select opportunities in credit sectors. We also believe agency MBS valuations are attractive. Additionally high-quality, short-duration structured credit offers strong relative value and risk/reward profiles.

Data presented reflects past performance. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be higher or lower than the performance shown. To obtain performance data current to the most recent month end, please visit www.americancentury.com/performance. Investment return and share value will fluctuate, and redemption value may be more or less than original cost. Data assumes reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. For information about other share classes available, please consult the prospectus. There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met. Dividends and yields represent past performance and there is no guarantee that they will continue to be paid.

You should consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses carefully before you invest. The fund’s prospectus or summary prospectus, which can be obtained at American Century Investments® Home, contains this and other information about the fund, and should be read carefully before investing.

The opinions expressed are those of the portfolio investment team and are no guarantee of the future performance of any American Century Investments portfolio. Statements regarding specific holdings represent personal views and compensation has not been received in connection with such views. This information is for an educational purpose only and is not intended to serve as investment advice.

The information is not intended as a personalized recommendation or fiduciary advice and should not be relied upon for investment, accounting, legal or tax advice.

Investments in fixed income securities are subject to the risks associated with the debt securities including credit, price and interest rate risk.

In certain interest rate environments, such as when real interest rates are rising faster than nominal interest rates, inflation-protected securities with similar durations may experience greater losses than other fixed income securities. Interest payments on inflation-protected debt securities will fluctuate as the principal and/or interest is adjusted for inflation and can be unpredictable.

There is no guarantee that the investment objectives will be met.

Generally, as interest rates rise, the value of the securities held in the fund will decline. The opposite is true when interest rates decline.

Source: Bloomberg Index Services Ltd. Bloomberg U.S. 1-5 Year Treasury Inflation Protected Securities(TIPS) Index consists of inflation protected securities issued by the U.S. Treasury with maturities between one and five years.

Non-FDIC Insured | May Lose Value | No Bank Guarantee

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities(Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities(ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points(BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for“carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations(CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations(CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities(CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index(CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration(CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or“cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets(EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund(ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee(FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve(Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts(Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation(GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product(GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises(GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield(HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade(IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A“market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI(All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into"pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures(PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing(QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts(REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard“swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities(TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct"full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes(VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any"inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tompkins Financial: Raising Earnings Estimate And Upgrading To Buy After Q4 Surprise
data.description:
In my last report on Tompkins Financial Corporation (NYSE:TMP), which I wrote in November 2024, I downgraded the company to a hold rating. Since then, the stock price has dropped by ~6%. Also, the company has released its fourth quarter’s earnings results, which beat my expectations. I’m now expecting the company’s earnings to continue to grow on the back of normal loan and non-interest income growth. I’m expecting an EPS of $5.51 for 2025, up 11% year-over-year. I’ve raised my earnings estimate for 2025 mostly because the balance sheet grew more than expected in the last quarter. Based on my updated total expected return, I’m upgrading Tompkins Financial to a buy rating.

1Q’s Margin to Benefit from 4Q’s Rate Cuts

Tompkins Financial’s net interest margin was 14 basis points higher in the last quarter of 2024 compared to the third quarter, which beat my expectations. The margin’s improvement was attributable to a drop in the average cost of funds. The management explained in the earnings release that the lower funding cost was a result of a growth in average deposits and lower market rates.

Going forward, I’m expecting the margin to continue to expand in the first quarter because of the Fed funds rate cuts in November and December 2024. The average loan yield will remain sticky, while the average deposit cost will re-price downward in the near term. Tompkins Financial’s loan yield is sticky because its loan book is heavy on real estate loans, which usually carry fixed rates. The following chart shows the loan composition as of September 2024 as December’s data isn’t available as of the writing of this report.

Meanwhile, the average deposit cost is sensitive to rate changes because of the large portion of interest-bearing checking, savings, and money market accounts.

There is currently a lot of uncertainty regarding the Fed funds rate changes in upcoming months, so I’m assuming that the interest rates will remain unchanged through 2025 from the end of 2024. As a result, after the first quarter, I’m expecting the margin to be somewhat stable. I’m expecting the margin to grow by 6 basis points in 1Q 2025 and remain unchanged thereafter.

My net interest margin estimate for 2025 is higher than my previous estimate because the fourth quarter’s margin expansion beat my expectations, so the base is higher.

Loan Growth Deceleration Ahead After Incredible Year

Tompkins Financial finished 2024 in a strong manner with loan growth of 2.4% in the last quarter, which led to full-year loan growth of 7.4%. Last year’s loan growth is better than Tompkins’ historical five-year compounded annual growth rate of 4.1%.

I think growth will likely decelerate this year and approach the historical mean. Firstly, I think there is too much uncertainty related to tariffs and counter tariffs, which will deter corporations from making investment and business expansion decisions in the near term.

My loan growth outlook is also based on the existing economic condition of Tompkins Financial’s markets. The company, through its subsidiary Tompkins Community Bank, serves individuals and businesses in the Central, Western, and Hudson Valley regions of New York and the Southeastern region of Pennsylvania. Commercial Real Estate is the biggest loan segment at about 56% of total loans, while Residential Real Estate is the second largest at about 27%.

Since my last report on TMP in November 2024, New York’s unemployment rate (excluding NYC) has declined while Pennsylvania’s unemployment rate has grown very slightly.

Meanwhile, my outlook for residential real estate loans is worse than before because previously I was quite confident about Fed funds rate cuts in 2025. Now I’m not certain because the Federal Reserve seems to be in a wait and monitor mode. High borrowing costs will deter homebuyers. Moreover, the house price indices of the two states continue to be as steep as before.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan book to grow by 3.6% in 2025, which is close to the 5-year CAGR of 4.1%. I’m assuming that TMP will be able to fund its loans through its deposits without having to resort to borrowings, so I’m expecting deposits to grow in line with loans.

Although I haven’t raised my loan growth estimate for 2025, my loan balance estimate is higher than my previous estimate because the loan growth in the last quarter exceeded my expectations. I’m putting the same growth rate on a higher base now.

Other Financial Businesses Likely to Continue to Remain Stable

Apart from Tompkins Community Bank, Tompkins Financial has two other subsidiaries: Tompkins Insurance Agencies and Tompkins Financial Advisors. Wealth management fee income from Tompkins Financial Advisors has been quite stable in the past, as can be seen in the chart below. Interest rate ups and downs and stock market rallies and slumps have not had much of an impact on Tompkins’ wealth management fee income over the last ten years, which shows that it is quite immune to market conditions. Therefore, I’m expecting this component of non-interest income to remain stable in 2025.

As shown in the chart above, income from the insurance business has grown steadily since 2019. Insurance volume is usually not affected by business cycles because individuals and businesses still need insurance in bad times. I believe this business can continue to grow at the historical rate this year.

Overall, I’m expecting the non-interest income to grow by 2% year-over-year in 2025.

Raising the Earnings Estimate to $5.51

Earnings of Tompkins Financial will likely receive support from normal loan and non-interest income growth this year. Further, the first quarter’s earnings will benefit from net interest margin expansion.

I’m assuming the growth of provision expenses for loan losses will be commensurate with loan growth. Further, I’m assuming that non-interest expense growth will be commensurate with revenue growth.

As a result, I’m estimating earnings per share of $5.51 for 2025, up 11% from 2024. In my last report on Tompkins Financial, I estimated an EPS of $4.96. I’ve raised my earnings estimate partly because I’ve increased both my margin and loan balance estimates for this year.

Upgrading to Buy

Tompkins Financial’s current quarterly dividend level of $0.62 per share leads to a dividend yield of 3.6%. My EPS estimate and the DPS suggest a payout ratio of 45% for 2025, which is higher than the last five-year average (excluding 2023) of 41%. Therefore, I’m not expecting a dividend hike.

Tompkins Financial’s historical price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples are not meaningful because of the loss-making years.

Therefore, I’m using the peer average multiples to value Tompkins Financial.

TMP AMTB CTBI BFC CNOB BHRB Peer Average
P/E ("ttm") 14.02 NM 12.04 15.61 15.26 22.73 16.41
P/E ("fwd") 12.51 11.95 10.96 14.68 18.61 8.57 12.95
P/B ("ttm") 1.40 1.12 1.32 1.59 0.91 1.33 1.25
P/TB ("ttm") 1.62 1.15 1.45 2.29 1.12 1.52 1.51

Source: Seeking Alpha

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my forecast for EPS gives a target price of $71.4.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my forecast for the tangible book value per share gives a target price of $77.2.

The average target price for December 2025 comes out to $74.3, which suggests a 6.6% upside from the February 17 closing market price. In my last report, I determined a target price of $74.6 for December 2025 and adopted a hold rating. Although I have raised my EPS estimate, my new target price is slightly lower than before because of the following two reasons.

  1. The peer average P/E multiple has declined.
  2. The tangible book value per share (and book value per share) for the end of December 2024 was much lower than my previous expectation because of some Other Comprehensive Income line items that I was not expecting. Therefore, even though I’ve raised my earnings estimate (and maintained the dividend estimate), my new tangible book value per share estimate is much lower than my previous estimate.

Since my last report, which I wrote in November 2024, the stock price has dipped by around 6%, which has opened up some room for upside.

Adding my new expected price upside to the dividend yield gives a total expected return of 10.2%. As this return is not bad, I’m upgrading Tompkins Financial to a buy rating.

data.title: GUSA: Nothing Special About This 1000-Stock Large Cap ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Goldman Sachs MarketBeta U.S. 1000 Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:GUSA) is designed to provide core market-cap-weighted exposure to the large-cap segment of the U.S. stock market at a low cost. With strong similarities to the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), there's nothing necessarily wrong with the approach, but the large-cap segment can be divided up several different ways, and the purpose of this article is to determine which way is optimal. In this article, I'll review GUSA against IWB and three other large-cap funds of different concentrations and state my opinion that GUSA is a solid but probably not optimal solution in the large-cap segment. I hope you enjoy the read.

GUSA Overview

Strategy Discussion and Peer Group

The GUSA website names broad market exposure, use as a core position, and low cost as the three main reasons to consider owning. Launched on April 5, 2022, GUSA tracks the Solactive GBS United States 1000 Index, whose methodology is summarized below.

1. Common stocks, REITs, trading stocks, and units are eligible, provided they trade on a qualifying exchange (NYSE, NYSE MKT, NYSE Arca, NASDAQ, Investors, BATS).

2. Securities are ranked by total market capitalization, with the top 850 automatically qualifying. A buffer rule allows current components ranked 851-1200 to qualify until the total number of Index stocks reaches 1000.

3. The Index is free-float market-cap-weighted and rebalances the first Wednesday of February, May, August, and November, with the selection date 20 days prior.

The Index is similar to other 1000-stock funds like IWB, the Vanguard Russell 1000 Index Fund ETF (VONE), and the Schwab 1000 ETF (SCHK). As a result, GUSA tracks them quite well. Since its inception, GUSA has delivered a total return of 40.09%, and its path to get there has been nearly identical.

GUSA's 0.11% expense ratio is good, but it ranks below the other three ETFs in my rankings because of relatively poor liquidity. While the best measure of liquidity of an ETF is the liquidity of its underlying holdings, which is obviously about the same, GUSA has a high 0.25% 30-day median bid-ask spread, per its fund page. This one-time cost won't matter much for long-term holders, but still, there's no real advantage in choosing GUSA over the others.

Instead, I want to explore ETFs representing different cuts of the large-cap segment. I've chosen three to analyze alongside GUSA and IWB, as follows:

  1. iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV)
  2. iShares Russell Top 200 ETF (IWL)
  3. iShares S&P 100 ETF (OEF)

IWL and OEF aren't nearly as well-established as IVV, and their expense ratios possibly reflect that. However, their progressively higher concentrations mean their weighted average market caps are higher, with more emphasis placed on mega-cap stocks and less on smaller, perhaps more speculative names.

Performance Analysis

Using IWB as a proxy for GUSA, we see that IVV, IWL, and OEF have delivered stronger returns over the last three-, five-, and ten-year periods, with volatility (standard deviation) about the same for all funds. OEF was the best performer, and that's because of the dominance of mega-cap stocks, particularly the Magnificent Seven, in recent years.

For those not convinced, the M7's abnormal returns will continue, it's worth noting OEF's weakest five-year period since October 2009 still delivered a 7.80% annualized return compared to 6.68%, 7.75%, and 6.10% for IVV, IWL, and IWB, respectively.

IVV looks like a solid "in-between" product, as it seems to have better "low" returns on a one-year basis, but it certainly questions the need for ultra-diversified funds like GUSA and IWB. They don't even add that much diversification either, as GUSA has 90.53% overlap with IVV despite having 500 more holdings.

GUSA Analysis

Allocation Differences vs. Peer Group

GUSA's top 25 holdings are listed below, which total 45.24% of the portfolio. I've also listed each stock's allocation in IWB, IVV, IWL, and OEF, and you can see that the biggest jump in concentration is between IWL and OEF (58.31% to 71.30%). This shows the influence mega-cap stocks have on the broader stock market. For example, the Magnificent Seven (AAPL, NVDA, MSFT, AMZN, GOOGL, META, TSLA) have a 29.21% allocation in GUSA compared to 46.08% in OEF. How you view this group of stocks should drive which fund you prefer.

From a sector allocation perspective, the main impact is greater exposure to the Industrials and Real Estate sectors for GUSA at the expense of Communication Services and Technology. More specifically, GUSA overweights stocks in the Building Products and Industrial Machinery & Supplies & Components sub-industries, but its exposure to semiconductor stocks increases as you move from GUSA to IVV to IWL to OEF.

Fundamental Analysis By Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for GUSA's top 25 holdings. See how the summary metrics at the bottom change as you increase concentration all the way to OEF, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. To reiterate, there is little separating GUSA and IWB. The reason they have delivered nearly identical returns is because their composition and fundamentals are close on every metric. They both have similar weighted average market caps of about $1 trillion, trade at about 23x forward earnings, offer about 16.5% one-year estimated earnings per share growth, and have net margins of around 20.50%. This is unlikely to change, so it really comes down to fund provider preference and liquidity, of which I give IWB the advantage.

2. As you move from GUSA to OEF, notice how valuations increase, and that is because the stocks at the top of the list (e.g., Apple, Nvidia) trade at elevated valuations and have higher weights. Overall, GUSA trades at 22.97x forward earnings, or about 5.5% cheaper than OEF, but the offset is lower growth. GUSA's one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates of 11.00% and 16.51% are 1-2% lower, and again, this is due to lower mega-cap growth stock exposure, especially Nvidia.

3. A key drawback with the greater cap-weighted diversification is weaker quality. For example, GUSA's 20.41% weighted average net margins are nearly 4% lower than OEF's, and as shown below, GUSA's margins are lower across the board.

In addition, GUSA's weighted average debt-to-free cash flow ratio is 5.28x compared to 4.73x for OEF, and its 33.11x interest coverage ratio is almost seven points less, suggesting its components are less able to handle an interest rate increase. This scenario is possible, given the latest CPI report showed the highest month-over-month readings since March 2024 and the ongoing uncertainty regarding President Trump's tariff policies.

Investment Recommendation

GUSA has no advantage or disadvantage over other 1,000-stock funds like IWB, except for a high 0.25% bid-ask spread, which could increase trading costs. However, it's not the best choice for the large-cap segment, given how IVV, IWL, and OEF have stronger quality features, similar volatility, and acceptable valuations for the growth rates offered. For these reasons, I've assigned GUSA a neutral "hold." Thank you for reading.

data.title: Occidental Petroleum: Making Cost Progress
data.description:
The last article on Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY)(NEOE:OXY:CA) noted that Warren Buffett and other great investors will buy when the stock is a bargain and not worry if the stock goes lower. Warren Buffett in fact purchased still more stock at a lower price as the article noted. Now, the fourth quarter earnings noted that cash flow was roughly the same even though commodity prices declined. That likely means that the acquisitions were worth every penny when it comes to cost reductions. It also means that when prices recover, this company will be making more money. When that is combined with the ongoing cost reduction and efficiency drives, it means that record earnings will come at lower peak oil prices than occurred the last time around. Occidental is likely now more of a bargain than it was when Warren Buffett first began to build a position in Occidental Petroleum.

Commodity Prices

Shown below are the commodity prices received for the time period shown:

What is interesting is that oil prices declined as shown above, and normally oil prices have the most visible effect on earnings. However, natural gas prices increased sharply in the fourth quarter and are likely to show a significant increase in the first quarter because this winter has been unusually cold for the better part of the quarter.

Should natural gas prices join the much stronger world market as export capacity increases, this company would benefit in the future from the natural gas production exposure. Texas probably has the largest surplus of natural gas compared to the demand that midstream is able to get the natural gas to. Therefore, any kind of improvement is a welcome profit improvement. Between growing data center demand, increasing export capacity, and exports to Mexico, the outlook for natural gas is increasing bright.

Trending Results

That may well mean that the focus solely upon weak oil prices may not tell the whole profit story here. That is in addition to the effect of lower cost production that the acquisitions have added.

At least some of the increasing cash flow is due to production increases from the acquisitions. But cash flow increased the last two quarters far in excess of the increase from acquisition production. Note that operating cash flow before working capital needs basically held steady. The previous fiscal year cash flow was boosted by money released from working capital.

Even with the increasing natural gas prices, that is an incredibly strong working capital performance overall. As the company continues to assimilate and optimize the recent acquisitions, there could well be more improvements to come.

The difference between the cash flow performance and the earnings performance came largely from a court ruling that caused an impairment charge. Since impairment charges do not immediately affect cash flow, the operating cash flow results were not affected.

Reserve Replacement

Continuing replacement of reserves also shows a bright picture going well into the future:

This management has long discussed the process of adding Tier 1 locations. As a side note, this same process goes on throughout the industry. That means that the industry is in no danger of running out of Tier 1 locations.

The overall results are shown above, with an industry-leading finding and development cost of $10.70 per barrel. The acquisitions raised that cost somewhat. But management likely has upside potential ideas for the acquisitions to bring the average cost down over the long term.

Keep in mind that the reserves shown above are producible reserve using currently available technology. That is a fraction of the oil in place. That fraction could grow as technology continues to advance. Generally, the oil in place far exceeds what technology enables the companies to produce. This acreage could keep Occidental busy for a long time to come that far exceeds current management projections.

Acreage Advantage

Occidental has long produced some of the largest wells in the business because the acreage it has is some of the most productive.

The key here is that Occidental was active in the Permian long before the Permian became a household word. Despite all the news about the cost of acquisitions, the company owns a lot of acreage that it has had for ages that likewise competes for capital. That acreage was acquired at a far lower cost than many competitors paid.

In addition to the fact that the wells produce better (on cheap acreage) due to that "first mover" location advantage, the company has very profitable wells as shown above.

This company is not about to overpay for an acquisition because it already has likely the best location selections in the industry. Instead, management is looking for acquisitions to add to that premier position. Right now, it would appear that is what is happening from the cash flow performance.

Summary

As the article title notes, a fair amount of cost improvement happened in the current fiscal year.

Operating costs dropped as technology continues to advance, and the company learned more about the acreage acquired recently. But those lower costs also allowed for a reserve increase before acquisitions.

Note that management will be disposing of more non-core acreage to get the debt levels down still more. The very cold winter could give natural gas prices a further boost. That could very much improve first quarter expectations if the cold weather bouts continue. La Nina could provide a further boost to earnings because La Nina summers are usually very hot. That could mean still more above-average natural gas demand this summer.

The development decisions and the resulting corporate profitability likely come from the absolute amounts of oil produced in the six- and twelve-month period. More than a few investors focus upon the production mix. But in the past, it has really been the amount of oil produced that has largely determined the development priority. Anytime the amount of oil produced is more than 100,000 barrels in six months (or even 12 months), the operator likely has a very profitable situation in the current pricing environment.

The key here is that Occidental keeps improving the results to reduce costs per barrel equivalent. As long as that continues, the corporate breakeven will drop. There will, therefore, be superior profitability performance at all commodity pricing points. It will not take as much of a commodity price rally to outperform past record earnings.

This reinforces that Occidental Petroleum remains a strong buy. Management is doing what it can to improve corporate profitability. Sometimes this improvement is hard for shareholders to see because commodity prices are rarely the same in any two years. However, as the strong cash flow performance showed above despite declining oil commodity prices, there is evidence of cost performance improvement. That is likely to continue for some time to come.

Growth in production per share is likely to continue to come from mostly accretive acquisitions and somewhat from production outperformance.

Risks

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn of commodity prices could materially affect the future of the company.

Acquisitions could fail to meet management expectations. Expected upside potential could fail to occur.

Occidental, like much of the industry, depends upon continuing technology advances to reduce operating costs. Those technology advances can stop at any time.

A large company like Occidental is less likely to suffer from the loss of personnel services. However, there may be an unusually talented individual somewhere in the organization whose loss of services could make a difference in the company's future.

data.title: ING Groep: An Ambitious ROE Target Of Over 14% For 2027
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past year, ING Groep (NYSE:ING) has marginally outperformed the iShares MSCI Europe Financials ETF (EUFN), delivering a total return of ~41%:

I previously covered ING back in January 2024 with a Hold rating, arguing selling covered calls offered an attractive way to generate extra income which could mitigate the impact of declining net interest income /NII/ on ING's profitability. Since then, ING and European financials have significantly exceeded my expectations, posting mid-double-digit total returns.

In contrast to the strong stock performance, ING's profitability declined in 2024 from the exceptional results achieved in 2023, with a further drop in profits forecast for 2025, driven in part by the sale of the onshore Russia business. Looking further ahead, ING unveiled ambitious 2027 targets, with the return on equity set to improve thanks to business growth, continued fee income expansion, and a smaller capital buffer. Even so, the better outlook is already reflected in ING's share price, and I confirm my Hold rating for the shares.

Company Overview

You can access all ING results on its website here. ING reports results in two key segments, Retail Banking and Wholesale Banking, with Retail Banking accounting for 67% of the bank's loan book:

ING remains one of the most net interest income-reliant European lenders, with NII accounting for 79% of revenues, leaving a 21% contribution to fee and commission income (if we ignore the volatile Other income category).

2024 Results Recap

2024 was a decent year for ING, with 11.5% growth in fee and commission income helping offset the 6% drop in net interest income. Ultimately though, the normalization in the cost of risk resulted in the bank's net result dropping 12.3% Y/Y to €6.4 billion, with the return on equity still at a comfortable 13% (2023: 14.8%):

Shareholders' equity stood at €16.25/share at the end of 2024. I estimate tangible shareholders' equity was about €15.85/share. The bank remains well capitalized with a CET1 capital ratio of 13.6% at the end of 2024, well above the 10.76% regulatory requirement.

2025 Outlook

The bank expects revenues to be roughly flat in 2025, with growth in commissions offsetting the marginal decline in NII. Expenses are forecast to rise by 4% in 2025, slightly below the 4.8% increase in 2024. Return on equity is expected above 12%, although it most notably excludes the €0.7 billion post-tax hit from the sale of ING's onshore Russia business (set to close in Q3 2025). The sale will also decrease CET1 capital by 0.05%.

Overall I would say that ING's outlook appears achievable although the bank will likely miss its revenue target if the ECB cuts interest rates more than currently anticipated.

2027 Ambitions

ING targets a return on equity greater than 14% in 2027, driven by growth in fees to €5 billion (up 25% relative to 2024, or about 7.6% per year) which together with 1 million in incremental mobile primary customers per year in Retail banking should help offset the lingering impact of ECB rate cuts. In total, revenues are forecast to grow 4-5% between 2024 and 2027.

The CET1 ratio is forecast at 12.5%, which will also help in reaching the profitability target due to the denominator effect (dividing ING's profit by a smaller number).

All in all, while I am a bit skeptical about ING's ability to sustain the high level of fee growth, which is well above nominal GDP growth in the Eurozone, loan growth (~1.8% in 2024), and customer growth, the bank may well achieve its 2027 target as the key driving factor will be the abovementioned denominator effect, i.e. running a small capital buffer relative to regulatory requirements. That said, risks remain to the downside, as virtually all European banks are striving to offset the drop in NII with fee income - something which is clearly not achievable at the aggregate level for all banks.

Valuation

ING trades at 1.05x its tangible book value, which in light of the bank's profitability targets and the expected hit from the onshore Russia business sale points to a very low double-digit return outlook in 2025-2027. EPS excluding Russia should be ~€2.00/share in 2025, putting the forward P/E multiple at 8.3x which on the one hand is not prohibitively expensive relative to the trailing P/E ratio of 11.3x for EUFN. On the other hand, ING's NII-reliance makes the bank dependent on continued fee income growth for incremental profitability improvement.

To conclude, despite the bank's ambitious 2027 targets, I believe there are better opportunities among other European financials and confirm my Hold rating for the shares. I would add the caveat that ING may be a Buy if you compare it with U.S. financials, but I reckon sticking to the European peer group is most prudent. There I think lenders such as ABN AMRO (OTCPK:AAVMY) which I covered here should do better.

Given the Hold rating and the near-term profitability hit from Russia, I reckon investors can generate extra income by selling covered calls, with the €17-18 December 2025 strike prices offering annualized returns of 6.2% and 3.9% respectively.

Risks

The main risk in the investment case is the continued normalization of ECB monetary policy, with most forecasters agreeing that rates will decline to 2% in 2025. Any sustained decline below that level will likely derail ING's long-term profitability ambitions and lead to the bank missing its return targets. The other key risk to monitor is whether ING reaches its elevated fee income growth targets in the face of stiff competition from European peers. The good news here is that fee growth actually accelerated in Q4 2024, to 13.9% Y/Y from 11.5% for the full year 2024, putting ING on a good trajectory going into 2025.

Conclusion

ING delivered a return on equity of 13% in 2024, below the stellar 14.8% in 2023. Reported ROE is likely to dip further in 2025 as the bank will face headwinds from its Russia business sale and ECB rate cuts. Looking ahead to 2027, profitability is set to recover, driven by growth in fee income and a smaller capital buffer (denominator effect). Even so, I do not think a repeat of the exceptional results of 2023 will happen any time soon as they included an abnormally low risk result.

ING's valuation is not prohibitively expensive relative to broad European financials held in the EUFN ETF, or U.S. financials for that matter. That said, I believe there are better opportunities among other European banks that trade at even lower valuations and/or do not share ING's excessive NII reliance. As such, I confirm my Hold rating for the shares.

Thank you for reading.
